content.text: Biodiversity refers to the variety of plants and animals found in a particular region. Every member played a different role, and plants and animals are deeply interdependent for food, shelter, and reproduction.

content.title: A Nature Walk
procedure: Observe plants, insects, birds, and animals in a park or forest.
Note features like stem type, leaf shape, flower colour, and animal movement.
Record observations in a structured table.
content.observation: Nature is filled with diverse smells, sounds, and structures (grasses, bushes, trees).

content.text: Grouping (classification) makes it easier to understand and study the world by organizing things based on similarities and differences.

content.title: Sorting by Features
procedure: Collect pictures of various plants and animals.
Group them based on common features like height, color, habitat, or movement.
content.conclusion: Criteria for grouping can vary (e.g., someone might group by height, others by the presence of flowers).

content.title: Other Forms
content.text: Climbers use support to grow upwards, while Creepers spread along the ground.
The pattern of veins on a leaf is called venation.

items: Reticulate Venation: Net-like pattern on both sides of a middle vein (e.g., Hibiscus, Chickpea).
Parallel Venation: Veins run parallel to each other (e.g., Banana, Grass, Wheat).

content.title: Root Types
content.text: Taproot: One main root with smaller side roots (e.g., Mustard). Fibrous Roots: A bunch of similar-sized thin roots (e.g., Grass).
Seeds can be grouped based on the number of cotyledons (seed leaves).

items: Dicotyledons (Dicots): Seeds with two cotyledons (e.g., Chickpea/Chana).
Monocotyledons (Monocots): Seeds with one cotyledon (e.g., Maize).

content.title: Important Corelations
content.text: Dicots usually have reticulate venation and taproots. Monocots usually have parallel venation and fibrous roots.
Animals use different body parts for movement (flying, running, crawling, hopping).
A habitat is the place where a plant or animal lives, providing food, water, air, and shelter.

items: Amphibians: Live on both land and water (e.g., Frogs).

content.text: Adaptations are special features that enable survival in a specific region.

content.title: Examples of Adaptations
items: Desert: Fleshy stems (Cactus) to store water. Camels have long legs/wide hooves (hot) vs short legs/2 humps (cold).
Mountains: Conical shapes and sloping branches (Deodar) to let snow slide off.
Oceans: Streamlined bodies in fish for easy swimming.

content.text: Janaki Ammal was a botanist who documented plant biodiversity and led the 'Save Silent Valley' movement. Salim Ali (Birdman of India) extensively studied bird habitats and helped preserve regions like Keoladeo National Park.

items: Project Tiger (1973): To protect declining Bengal Tiger populations.
Sacred Groves: Traditionally community-protected forest patches, rich in medicinal plants.

glossary.definition: The variety of living organisms in a particular region.
Special features that enable plants and animals to survive in a particular region.
The natural environment where a plant or animal lives.
The pattern of veins on a leaf (parallel or reticulate).
The part of a seed that provides food for the developing plant.
A community-protected forest patch with religious or traditional significance.

summary_points: Nature is filled with a vast diversity of plants and animals that depend on each other.
Grouping based on features like stem type, leaf venation, and root structure helps categorize plants.
Animals are grouped based on their movements and habitats.
Adaptations are essential features that help organisms survive in extreme environments like deserts and mountains.
Habitat damage is a major cause of biodiversity loss, making conservation efforts (like Project Tiger and Sacred Groves) vital.
